results.possibleIdentity: Nicholas Roukes (born 1929) is a Canadian artist, author, and art educator who was significantly active during the late 1960s and 1970s. He served as Professor Emeritus of Art at the University of Calgary in Alberta, Canada. Roukes is well known for his exploration of the intersection between art and technology, particularly through his pioneering work with modern materials such as plastics and acrylics. His publications, which were widely influential in art education, include 'Classroom Craft Manual' (1960), 'Sculpture in Plastics' (1968), 'Plastics for Kinetic Art' (1974), and 'Art Synectics' (1982). His work 'Sculpture in Plastics' specifically addresses the interaction between art and technology, a central theme of the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization. Roukes is associated with the historical period and themes of E.A.T., and his name 'Nicholas Roukes' is likely the historical identity intended by the phonetic spelling 'Nicklas Roches.'

results.possibleIdentity: Peter Aaron is a prominent American architectural photographer and filmmaker who has been active since the late 1960s. He graduated from Bard College in 1968, where he was a contemporary of figures such as Chevy Chase and captured extensive photographic documentation of campus life and students between 1964 and 1971. Aaron began his professional career as a cinematographer before transitioning to photography, eventually serving a transformational apprenticeship under the legendary architectural photographer Ezra Stoller. He became a principal photographer for the agency Esto (founded by Stoller) and his work has been featured extensively in publications like Architectural Digest and in numerous books on architecture and design. Aaron is noted for his association with the non-profit organization Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.); his photography has been used to document the organization's historic collaborative projects between artists and engineers, such as '9 Evenings: Theatre and Engineering' (1966) and 'ARTCASH' (1971), and his images appear in comprehensive retrospectives of E.A.T.'s work. His professional approach focuses on capturing a 'Platonic ideal' of structures, emphasizing compositional complexity and cinematic lighting.
